---
title: "Reveal.js Code Annotation Fragments"
subtitle: "Quarto Extension"
author:
  - name: "Mickaël CANOUIL, _Ph.D._"
    orcid: "0000-0002-3396-4549"
    url: "https://mickael.canouil.fr"
format:
  revealjs:
    output-file: index
    code-annotations: select
revealjs-plugins:
  - codefrag
---

# Introduction

## What is Codefrag?

This extension enables fragment-based navigation through code annotations in Reveal.js presentations.

- Navigate annotations using arrow keys.
- Annotations are treated as fragments.
- Synchronises with line highlighting when present.

## Installation

Install the extension using:

```bash
quarto add mcanouil/quarto-revealjs-codefrag
```

## Basic Setup

Add to your document YAML:

```yaml
format:
  revealjs:
    code-annotations: select
revealjs-plugins:
  - codefrag
```

# Sequential Annotations

## Code Annotations

Use arrow keys to step through each annotation.

```{.r}
library(dplyr)            # <1>

mtcars |>                 # <2>
  filter(mpg > 20) |>     # <3>
  select(mpg, cyl, hp) |> # <4>
  arrange(desc(mpg))      # <5>
```

1. Load the dplyr package for data manipulation.
2. Start with the mtcars dataset.
3. Filter rows where mpg is greater than 20.
4. Select only the columns we need.
5. Sort by mpg in descending order.

## Python Example

Annotations work with any language.

```{.python}
import polars as pl                    # <1>

df = pl.read_csv("data.csv")           # <2>
df = df.filter(                        # <3>
    pl.col("value") > 0
)
result = df.group_by("category").agg(  # <4>
    pl.col("value").mean()
)
```

1. Import Polars for data manipulation.
2. Read a CSV file into a DataFrame.
3. Filter rows where value is positive.
4. Group by category and compute the mean.

# Line Highlighting Sync

## Synced Annotations (Matching Lines)

Every highlight clone matches an annotation on the same lines, so they share a navigation step.

```{.r code-line-numbers="|1|3-5|6"}
library(ggplot2)         # <1>

ggplot(mtcars) +         # <2>
  aes(x = mpg, y = wt) + # <2>
  geom_point() +         # <2>
  theme_minimal()        # <3>
```

1. Load the ggplot2 package.
2. Build a scatter plot of mpg vs wt.
3. Apply a minimal theme.

## Partial Match (Unmatched Annotation)

Without a leading `|`, the first highlight step is the initial state.
Unmatched annotations appear at the end; matched pairs still sync.

```{.r code-line-numbers="1|3-5|6"}
library(ggplot2)         # <1>

ggplot(mtcars) +         # <2>
  aes(x = mpg, y = wt) + # <2>
  geom_point() +         # <2>
  theme_minimal()        # <3>
```

1. Load the ggplot2 package.
2. Build a scatter plot of mpg vs wt.
3. Apply a minimal theme.

# Mixed Fragments

## Annotations with Other Fragments

Text fragments appear first, then code annotations follow.

::: {.fragment}
This paragraph fades in as the **first** fragment.
:::

::: {.fragment}
This paragraph fades in as the **second** fragment.
:::

::: {.fragment}

```{.r}
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)    # <1>
summary(model)                          # <2>
plot(model, which = 1)                  # <3>
```

1. Fit a linear model predicting mpg from weight.
2. Print the model summary.
3. Plot residuals versus fitted values.

:::

::: {.fragment}
This paragraph fades in **after** all annotations.
:::

## Custom Fragment Indices

Use `code-annotation-fragment-indices` to interleave annotations with other fragments.

::: {.fragment fragment-index=1}
We need a data manipulation library before writing any pipeline code.
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=3}
With the data piped in, we can start applying transformations.
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=5}
The result keeps only cars that achieve more than 20 miles per gallon.
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=2}

```{.r code-annotation-fragment-indices="2,3,4"}
library(dplyr)            # <1>
mtcars |>                 # <2>
  filter(mpg > 20)        # <3>
```

1. Load dplyr for data wrangling.
2. Pipe the mtcars dataset into the next step.
3. Keep only rows where mpg exceeds 20.

:::

## Incremental List then Annotations

::: {.incremental}
- Import a data processing library.
- Scan the file lazily for efficiency.
- Collect the results into memory.
:::

::: {.fragment}

```{.python}
import polars as pl           # <1>
df = pl.scan_csv("train.csv") # <2>
df.collect()                  # <3>
```

1. Import the Polars library.
2. Create a lazy frame from CSV.
3. Materialise the lazy frame into memory.

:::

# Line Highlighting

## Line Highlights Only (No Annotations)

Line highlighting without annotations steps through highlights as normal fragments.

```{.r code-line-numbers="|1|3-4|6"}
library(dplyr)

mtcars |>
  filter(mpg > 20) |>

  summarise(mean_mpg = mean(mpg))
```

## Line Highlights with Annotations

All highlight clones match annotations on the same lines, so they sync.

```{.r code-line-numbers="|1|3-4|6"}
library(dplyr)                    # <1>

mtcars |>                         # <2>
  filter(mpg > 20) |>             # <2>

  summarise(mean_mpg = mean(mpg)) # <3>
```

1. Load the dplyr package.
2. Build the filtering pipeline.
3. Compute the summary statistic.

## Line Highlights with Custom Indices

Use `code-line-fragment-indices` to control which fragment index each highlight step receives.

::: {.fragment fragment-index=1}
A two-panel layout is set up to compare plots side by side.
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=3}
The first panel draws a basic scatter plot of speed vs distance.
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=5}
The second panel repeats the plot in red for comparison.
:::

```{.r code-line-numbers="|2|3" code-line-fragment-indices="1,2,4"}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(cars)
plot(cars, col = "red")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

## Line Highlights with Custom Indices and Annotations

Combines `code-line-fragment-indices` with annotations.

::: {.fragment fragment-index=1}
Define the input as a numeric sequence.
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=3}
Square each value to build a parabola.
:::

```{.r code-line-numbers="|1|3|5" code-line-fragment-indices="1,2,4,6"}
x <- 1:10         # <1>

y <- x^2

plot(x, y)        # <2>
```

1. Create a sequence from 1 to 10.
2. Plot the result.

## Line Highlights with Fragments Before

::: {.fragment}
This script generates a simple parabola.
Step through each highlighted line below.
:::

```{.r code-line-numbers="|1|3|5"}
x <- 1:10         # <1>

y <- x^2

plot(x, y)        # <2>
```

1. Create a sequence from 1 to 10.
2. Plot the result.

# Plain Fragments

## Fragments Without Annotations

Slides with plain fragments (no code annotations or line highlights) should print as a single page.

::: {.fragment}
First point appears.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Second point appears.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Third point appears.
:::

# Configuration

## Disabling the Plugin

Disable fragment navigation while keeping tooltips:

```yaml
extensions:
  codefrag:
    enabled: false
```

## Features {.smaller}

1. **Sequential Fragments**: Annotations become navigable fragments.
2. **Line Highlight Sync**: Matching annotations and highlights share a step.
3. **Partial Match**: Matched pairs sync; unmatched items get their own steps.
4. **Custom Indices**: Control fragment order with custom index attributes.
5. **Tooltip Patching**: Tooltips reposition to avoid clipping.
6. **Bidirectional Navigation**: Navigate forwards and backwards.

# Thank You

## Questions?

Feel free to ask any questions about the Reveal.js Code Annotation Fragments extension.
